name: hospice_morphine_double_effect_family_objection
premise: Hospice medical director Dr. Sarah Kim must decide whether to continue morphine titration for a terminally ill patient when the patient's adult son revokes prior comfort-focused consent, citing fears that opioids hasten death. Nurse manager Luis Ortega documents clear distress and air hunger; palliative guidelines support continuing titration with documented intent to relieve suffering. Court intervention would take days the patient may not have; stopping opioids may leave conscious suffocation.

agents.name: Dr. Sarah Kim
agents.identity: I am the hospice medical director balancing patient comfort, surrogate conflict, and legal risk in end-of-life opioid management.
agents.goal: I want to relieve suffering without crossing into intentional hastening that the family could litigate as euthanasia.

agents.name: Luis Ortega
agents.identity: I am the nurse manager at bedside documenting symptom escalation and family dynamics during morphine titration decisions.
agents.goal: I want whatever stops the patient from drowning in their own secretions tonight, even if that upsets the son's moral framework.

choices: I will pause titration and reduce to prior baseline while convening an emergency ethics consult and chaplain mediation, accepting the risk of undertreated air hunger overnight.
I will continue titration strictly for documented dyspnea per protocol and bar the son from medication decisions under clinical override rules, accepting legal exposure if statutes favor surrogates.
forced_choice.call_to_action: The patient is alert between doses and clutching the bed rail. Dr. Sarah Kim must choose between pausing titration for mediation or continuing titration under dyspnea protocol. What does Dr. Sarah Kim do?
